Recovery Services vaults are concentrated in eastus and overwhelmingly use the RS0 SKU with infrastructure encryption enabled: this shows a consistent use of platform backup vaults with server-side encryption, so plan to provision a paired Recovery Services vault in a secondary region (e.g., westus2) and use the RS0 vault configuration with infrastructure encryption to meet geo-redundant backup requirements for on‑prem SQL.

Key Vaults have soft-delete (90 days) and purge-protection enabled and are frequently publicly accessible: because tenant vaults already protect keys from accidental deletion but allow broad network access, plan to store customer-managed keys (CMKs) in Key Vault with purge-protection and soft-delete while adding network restrictions (private endpoint or firewall rules) and enabling RBAC or controlled access for the managed identity used by Azure Backup.

A non-trivial portion of storage accounts use geo-redundant SKUs (Standard_GRS 23.3% and Standard_RAGRS 17.2%): this indicates an existing preference for geo-redundant storage, so plan backup storage for on‑prem SQL retention using GRS/RA-GRS redundancy or vault-level geo-redundant backup storage to ensure cross-region durability.

Recovery Services vault identities are split with ~50% using user-assigned managed identities and many tenant resources already using user-assigned identities: since user-assigned identities are common, plan to assign a dedicated user-assigned managed identity to the backup vault and automation runbooks so they can be granted least‑privilege access to Key Vault CMKs and other resources.

Automation accounts and runbooks are present but limited (3 automation accounts, 10 runbooks) while Logic workflows are commonly used with user-assigned identities: because automation tooling exists but is not widespread, plan to implement monthly DR test orchestration using a new or existing Automation account or Logic App with a user-assigned identity (reusing tenant identity patterns) and integrate scheduled triggers to execute DR test runbooks.

Public network access is enabled for the vast majority of Recovery Services vaults (98.9%) and most Key Vaults/storage accounts also allow public access: given the current permissive network posture, plan to harden backup endpoints by restricting vault and Key Vault network access (private endpoints, service endpoints, or firewall rules) and validate connectivity from on‑prem backup appliances during DR test runs.

Tenant resources show strong adoption of TLS 1.2 minimums (storage minimum TLS1_2 = 96.6%, SQL servers minimum TLS1.2 = 85.7%): this indicates a baseline of encrypted transport, so plan to require TLS 1.2 for all backup agents, on‑prem to Azure connections, and endpoint configuration used in automated DR tests.

Key Vault RBAC is not yet broadly enabled (enablerbacauthorization False on ~60% of vaults) while purge protection and soft delete are universally enabled: because key access is often managed via legacy access policies, plan to document and implement precise access controls (either enable RBAC where appropriate or explicitly define access policies) for the CMK used by Azure Backup to ensure recoverable, auditable key usage during automated DR tests.

TrafficManager/trafficmanagerprofiles and global DNS-based routing are widely present (294 profiles, global location): since tenant already uses global traffic routing, plan to reuse Traffic Manager or DNS failover records as part of DR test orchestration to validate application failover behavior after restoring on‑prem SQL backups into Azure environments.
